hook: If you died tomorrow, your state would decide what happens to everything you've worked for.
body:
If you died tomorrow, your state would decide what happens to everything you've worked for. Your home, your savings, your car — a court would sort it out using a one-size-fits-all formula that has nothing to do with your wishes or your family's needs.

A will is the single most direct way to change that. It puts your name on the decisions: who gets what, who handles your affairs, and how the people you love are protected. Without one, those decisions belong to someone else.

Most people assume they'll get around to it. The ones who don't are the ones whose families end up in probate court wondering what went wrong. Don't leave yours guessing.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.

hook: Most adult children don't find out their parents had no plan until it's too late to fix it.
body:
Most adult children don't find out their parents had no plan until it's too late to fix it. A parent passes, and suddenly the family is sorting through decades of paperwork, disagreeing over who gets what, and navigating a probate process that can drag on for months — or years.

It's not a comfortable conversation to start. But asking your parents whether they have a will, a power of attorney, and a named beneficiary on their accounts is one of the most caring things you can do right now. Not when a crisis hits — now, while there's still time to act.

If you're not sure where your family stands, start with an honest look at what's in place and what's missing. That clarity alone can change everything.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.

hook: If something happened to you and your partner today, who would raise your kids?
body:
If something happened to you and your partner today, who would raise your kids? Not who you'd hope for, or who you've mentioned in passing — who would a court actually appoint? Without a will that names a guardian, that decision goes to a judge who doesn't know your family, your values, or what your children need.

Naming a guardian is one of the most important things a parent can do, and one of the most skipped. It feels heavy to think about. But it takes less time than you'd expect, and it means your kids end up with the person you actually trust — not whoever the court selects.

You don't have to have it all figured out. You just have to start.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.

hook: Probate is what happens to your estate when the courts take over — and it's slower, costlier, and more public than most people realize.
body:
Probate is what happens to your estate when the courts take over — and it's slower, costlier, and more public than most people realize. Your assets can be tied up for months. Your family may have to hire an attorney just to access a bank account. And in most states, the whole process becomes a matter of public record.

The good news is that probate isn't inevitable. The right planning — a properly structured will, named beneficiaries, and in some cases a trust — can keep most or all of your estate out of court entirely. Your family gets what you intended, without the wait and without the legal fees eating into it.

Knowing where you stand is the first step. A lot of people are closer to a solid plan than they think.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.

hook: A power of attorney isn't about death — it's about what happens when your parent is still alive but can't make decisions on their own.
body:
A power of attorney isn't about death — it's about what happens when your parent is still alive but can't make decisions on their own. A stroke, a fall, a diagnosis that comes on fast. Without this document in place, you may not be able to pay their bills, manage their accounts, or make medical decisions on their behalf — even as their child.

Getting legal authority to help a parent who hasn't signed a power of attorney can mean going to court for guardianship. That process takes time and money your family may not have in a crisis.

If your parents are in their 60s, 70s, or beyond and this document doesn't exist, it's worth a conversation now. Not as a scary topic — just as something that protects everyone.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.

hook: Life insurance isn't the morbid topic people make it out to be — it's just a financial plan for the people who depend on you.
body:
Life insurance isn't the morbid topic people make it out to be — it's just a financial plan for the people who depend on you. If you have young kids and one income disappears, what happens to the mortgage? To childcare? To the future you were building together?

But here's the part most families miss: life insurance only works if the rest of your estate plan is set up correctly. A policy with no named beneficiary, or one that names a minor child directly, can create serious legal headaches. The money may not reach your family the way you intended.

Getting the policy is step one. Making sure it connects properly to your will and your overall plan is what makes it actually work.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.

hook: A trust isn't just for wealthy families — it's a tool that gives you more control over what happens to your assets than a will alone can.
body:
A trust isn't just for wealthy families — it's a tool that gives you more control over what happens to your assets than a will alone can. With a properly funded trust, your estate can transfer to the people you love without going through probate, on your timeline, with conditions you set.

With headlines this week pointing to potential changes in how retirement accounts and capital gains are taxed, more people are thinking carefully about how their assets are structured — and whether their current plan still makes sense. A trust can be part of that answer.

You don't need to figure out the right structure on your own. But knowing where you stand — what you have, what's missing, and what to ask an attorney — puts you in a much stronger position.

Get your estate readiness score at willestateready.com — $21, 10 minutes.
